Cleanup and destroy order, AWS documentation links, and Terraform Registry references for Lambda Managed Instances.
Provision the aws_lambda_capacity_provider resource with correct attribute names, VPC config, scaling, and operator role.
Access Grafana via the public ALB, understand the provisioned Loki datasource and WAF dashboard, and write LogQL queries against WAF log streams.

import { Aside } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

<Aside type="note">
  Terraform code:
  [terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance).
  On first boot, EC2 `user_data` writes datasource YAML and dashboard JSON under
  `/opt/obs/grafana/`. Terraform renders `templates/user_data.sh.tftpl` and injects
  `templates/dashboards/*.json` as base64 so the instance files match the repo.
</Aside>

Grafana runs alongside Loki on the same EC2 host via Docker Compose. A public ALB forwards HTTP port 80 to Grafana port 3000. The Loki datasource and WAF dashboards are provisioned from those files at container startup — no manual setup required.

## Access

```bash frame="terminal"
terraform output grafana_url
# Prints: http://<alb-dns-name>
```

Open the URL in a browser. Default credentials: **admin / admin**. Grafana prompts for a password change on first login.

<Aside type="caution">
  The ALB security group restricts ingress to your deployer's current public IP by default (via
  `data.http` `checkip.amazonaws.com`). If your IP changes, update `alb_ingress_cidrs` in
  `terraform.tfvars` and run `terraform apply` to refresh the security group rule.
</Aside>

## Provisioned datasource

Grafana automatically connects to Loki at `http://loki:3100` using Docker Compose internal DNS. The datasource is set as the default and requires no manual configuration:

```yaml title="Provisioning excerpt (written by user_data.sh.tftpl)"
apiVersion: 1
datasources:
  - name: Loki
    type: loki
    uid: loki
    url: http://loki:3100
    access: proxy
    isDefault: true
```

## WAF dashboards (folder **WAF**)

**WAF Logs** — one **Logs** panel on `{source="waf"}` for full JSON lines (newest first).

**WAF overview** — common WAFv2-style views on the same stream:

- Log volume per minute
- Time series by **`action`** (ALLOW, BLOCK, COUNT, CAPTCHA, …)
- Time series by **`terminatingRuleId`**
- Top client IPs via `| json clientIp="httpRequest.clientIp"`
- HTTP methods via `| json method="httpRequest.httpMethod"`
- **Logs** panel filtered to **`action="BLOCK"`** for triage

Panels assume AWS WAFv2 JSON body shape. If a chart is empty, confirm your objects match that schema or adjust the LogQL `json` field paths in the dashboard (Explore is the quickest place to experiment).

## LogQL queries

Query WAF log streams in **Explore** (left sidebar → compass icon):

```text title="All WAF log lines"
{source="waf"}
```

```text title="BLOCK actions only"
{source="waf"} | json | action = "BLOCK"
```

```text title="Client IP filter"
{source="waf"} | json | line_format "{{.httpRequest.clientIp}} {{.action}} {{.httpRequest.uri}}"
```

```text title="Request count by action over time"
sum by (action) (count_over_time({source="waf"} | json [5m]))
```

## ALB health check

The ALB target group health check hits `GET /api/health` on port 3000. Grafana returns `200 OK` once the container is fully initialised. The EC2 Docker Compose `healthcheck` on the Loki service gates Grafana startup — expect 2–4 minutes from `terraform apply` completion before Grafana is reachable via the ALB.

## Restarting the stack on EC2

If you need to restart Compose manually (e.g. after updating config files):

```bash frame="terminal"
# Connect via SSM
aws ssm start-session --target "$(terraform output -raw obs_instance_id)"

# Inside the session
cd /opt/obs
docker compose down
docker compose up -d
```

Demo — WAF Logs to Grafana Loki
End-to-end demo — WAF access logs flow from S3 to a Node.js 24 Lambda Managed Instance function to Loki and are visualised in Grafana.

import { Aside, Steps, FileTree, Card, CardGrid } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

<Aside type="note">
  Terraform code:
  [terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance).
  All demo resources live under `examples/waf-loki/`.
</Aside>

This demo wires together every concept from the walkthrough into one running pipeline. WAF access log files land in S3, an S3 event triggers a **Node.js 24 Lambda Managed Instance (LMI) function** that decompresses and pushes each log line to **Grafana Loki** on EC2, and **Grafana** surfaces a live log stream behind a public ALB.

## What the stack builds

```text
  AWS WAF access logs
         │
         ▼
   Amazon S3 (bucket name you set in waf_logs_bucket_name)
         │ S3:ObjectCreated
         ▼
   Lambda (LMI, Node.js 24)      ←── lambda_managed_instance
         │ HTTP POST :3100
         ▼
   Loki (EC2, Docker Compose)
         │
         ▼
   Grafana :3000 ←── ALB (public, HTTP 80)
```

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="S3 trigger" icon="seti:json">
    Any `.gz` object put to the WAF log bucket fires an S3 event notification directly to the LMI
    Lambda. No SQS or EventBridge needed for this volume.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Lambda Managed Instance" icon="rocket">
    The Node.js 24 WAF ingest function runs on a `lambda_managed_instance` capacity provider with
    VPC placement and the LMI security group.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Loki on EC2" icon="linux">
    Monolithic Loki (Docker Compose) on a single `t3.small` in a private subnet. Chunks and index
    persist on the root EBS volume. Lambda reaches Loki on port 3100 via the LMI security group
    rule.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Grafana via ALB" icon="external">
    A public ALB forwards HTTP 80 to Grafana port 3000. Access is locked to your current deployer IP
    by default. Default credentials: **admin / admin**.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Apply

Create the WAF log S3 bucket (and any bucket policy WAF needs) **before** apply. Set `waf_logs_bucket_name` in `terraform.tfvars` to that bucket’s name.

```bash frame="terminal"
cd examples/waf-loki
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars
# edit: waf_logs_bucket_name = "<existing-bucket>"
terraform init
terraform plan
terraform apply
```

First apply takes several minutes: capacity provider creation, EC2 startup, Docker image pulls, and the Grafana ALB health check all happen sequentially.

## Check Grafana

```bash frame="terminal"
terraform output grafana_url
# Open http://<alb-dns> in a browser — admin / admin
```

The **WAF Logs** dashboard appears under the **WAF** folder. The `{source="waf"}` stream is empty until a log object lands in the bucket.

## Test the ingest path

```bash frame="terminal"
echo '{"timestamp":1735700000000,"action":"ALLOW","terminatingRuleId":"Default_Action","httpRequest":{"clientIp":"1.2.3.4","uri":"/","httpMethod":"GET"}}' \
  | gzip > /tmp/test.log.gz

aws s3 cp /tmp/test.log.gz \
  "s3://$(terraform output -raw waf_logs_bucket)/test/test.log.gz"
```

The Lambda triggers automatically. Within seconds the log line appears in Grafana Explore.

## Destroy

```bash frame="terminal"
terraform destroy
```

`terraform destroy` removes this stack’s resources (including the S3 event notification). It does **not** delete the WAF log bucket, which Terraform never creates.
How Loki runs in this demo — monolithic mode, Docker Compose on a private EC2, EBS-backed storage, and the push path from Lambda.

import { Aside } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

<Aside type="note">
  Terraform code:
  [terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance).
  EC2 bootstrap: `examples/waf-loki/templates/user_data.sh.tftpl` (dashboards:
  `templates/dashboards/`).
</Aside>

Loki runs in **monolithic mode** on a single `t3.small` EC2 instance in a private subnet. Docker Compose manages both Loki and Grafana on the same host.

## EC2 bootstrap

Terraform provisions the instance with `user_data` that:

1. Installs Docker (`dnf install -y docker`).
2. Downloads Docker Compose v2 from GitHub releases.
3. Writes all config files to `/opt/obs/`.
4. Runs `docker compose up -d`.

SSM Session Manager is enabled via an IAM instance profile (`AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore`), so you can connect without an SSH key:

```bash frame="terminal"
aws ssm start-session --target "$(terraform output -raw obs_instance_id)"
```

## Loki config (`loki-config.yaml`)

```yaml title="templates/user_data.sh.tftpl (Loki excerpt)"
auth_enabled: false

server:
  http_listen_port: 3100

common:
  instance_addr: 127.0.0.1
  path_prefix: /loki
  storage:
    filesystem:
      chunks_directory: /loki/chunks
      rules_directory: /loki/rules
  replication_factor: 1
  ring:
    kvstore:
      store: inmemory

schema_config:
  configs:
    - from: 2020-10-24
      store: tsdb
      object_store: filesystem
      schema: v13
      index:
        prefix: index_
        period: 24h
```

Chunks and TSDB index write to `/loki` inside the container, which maps to a Docker **named volume** backed by the EC2 **root EBS volume** (30 GB gp3, encrypted).

## Storage durability

| Event                                                        | Effect on Loki data                                                         |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Instance **reboot** or stop/start                            | Data retained — same EBS volume reattaches                                  |
| Instance **replacement** (Terraform taint, hardware failure) | Data lost — new root volume                                                 |
| S3 WAF log objects                                           | Always retained in the WAF log bucket; manual re-upload re-drives ingestion |

<Aside type="tip">
  If you need Loki data to survive instance replacement, configure the S3 storage backend instead
  (`storage_config.aws` with TSDB shipper). For this demo, EBS is sufficient and keeps the stack
  simple.
</Aside>

## Network path: Lambda → Loki

Lambda ENIs are placed in private subnets and carry the `aws_security_group.lmi` security group. The EC2 (`aws_security_group.obs`) allows inbound **TCP 3100** from `aws_security_group.lmi`:

```hcl title="examples/waf-loki/main.tf (excerpt)"
ingress {
  from_port       = 3100
  to_port         = 3100
  protocol        = "tcp"
  security_groups = [aws_security_group.lmi.id]
  description     = "Loki push from Lambda ENIs"
}
```

`LOKI_URL` is set on the WAF function to `http://<ec2-private-ip>:3100/loki/api/v1/push`. The IP is resolved at `terraform apply` time; no internal DNS is required.

## Checking Loki status

```bash frame="terminal"
# Inside SSM session on the EC2
docker compose -f /opt/obs/docker-compose.yaml ps
docker compose -f /opt/obs/docker-compose.yaml logs --tail 50 loki

# Loki ready endpoint
curl http://localhost:3100/ready
```

Orientation to Lambda Managed Instances — definition, EC2 rationale, three-step flow, how to use this walkthrough, then fundamentals via cards.

import { CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components"; import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro"; import HowItWorks3StepsDiagram from "@/components/HowItWorks3StepsDiagram.astro"; ## What is Lambda Managed Instances? **Lambda Managed Instances (LMI)** runs your function on dedicated capacity in your own instead of **Shared Lambda infrastructure** (the **multi-tenant** fleet). You still use the Lambda programming model; compute is **EC2 in your account**, wired through a **capacity provider**. Isolation and tenancy differ from default Lambda — see **[Tenancy & isolation](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/security/)**. ## Why EC2 as the compute layer? | Benefit | What it means | | --------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Flexibility** | Current-gen CPUs, Graviton, network-optimised options — matched to workload | | **Control** | Your VPC, subnets, security groups — not another customer’s fleet | | **Performance** | Configurable memory / vCPU ratios and bandwidth | | **Cost levers** | EC2 Savings Plans and RIs apply to underlying compute (management fee is separate) | ## How it works — 3 steps The figure above shows the **operational sequence** for LMI: 1. **Provision placement** — define a **capacity provider** (VPC, subnets, security groups, instance requirements, scaling limits). 2. **Wire the function** — attach **`capacity_provider_config`** on the Lambda to that provider. 3. **Publish** — a new **version** remains inactive until **managed instances** and **execution environments** are ready; it then becomes **ACTIVE**. Another function on the **same** provider may consume **existing** spare capacity instead of adding instances. **Fully managed fleet:** you configure VPC, provider, and function; Lambda operates **managed instances** (lifecycle, patching, routing, and fleet scaling policy on that surface are not yours to run like normal EC2). ## Using this walkthrough

## Overview

The walkthrough VPC lab provisions subnets and a security group. This module builds the **capacity provider** side: the operator IAM role, the **`aws_lambda_capacity_provider`** (VPC placement, instance requirements, scaling), and the service-linked role that lets Lambda manage EC2 fleet lifecycle.

The Lambda function itself — execution role, log group, and `aws_lambda_function` — lives in the next lab: [Lambda Managed Function](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/walkthrough/lambda/).

Sections follow the comment groups in **`examples/waf-loki/main.tf`**. Code blocks use **illustrative literals** so you can read the shape without tracing variables.

## Identity

The operator role uses a **`name_prefix`** and is wired into the capacity provider's **`permissions_config`**. The capacity provider has its own unique **`name`**.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — identity-related fields"
# aws_iam_role.operator
name_prefix = "demo-op-"

# aws_lambda_capacity_provider.this
name = "demo-capacity"

permissions_config {
  capacity_provider_operator_role_arn = "arn:aws:iam::111122223333:role/demo-op-abc123"
}
```

Further reading: [IAM roles](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/operator-role/) (execution vs operator).

## Capacity provider and scaling

**`instance_requirements`** constrains CPU architecture and optional allow/deny instance-type lists. **`capacity_provider_scaling_config`** sets mode and vCPU ceiling; in **`Manual`** mode a CPU target policy is added.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — instance_requirements"
  instance_requirements {
    architectures           = ["x86_64"]
    allowed_instance_types  = null
    excluded_instance_types = null
  }
```

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — Auto scaling (no CPU target policy)"
  capacity_provider_scaling_config {
    scaling_mode   = "Auto"
    max_vcpu_count = 16
  }
```

Further reading: [Placement & capacity](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/capacity-providers/), [Elasticity & CPU](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/scaling/), [Supported instance families](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/capacity-providers/#supported-instance-families).

## VPC placement

**`vpc_config`** lives on **`aws_lambda_capacity_provider`** (fleet ENIs). This is separate from any VPC config on an `aws_lambda_function`.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — vpc_config"
  vpc_config {
    subnet_ids         = ["subnet-0a1b2c3d", "subnet-0e5f6a7b"]
    security_group_ids = ["sg-0123456789abcdef0"]
  }
```

[VPC lab](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/walkthrough/networking/) wires **`aws_security_group.lmi`** and private subnets passed in here.

## Tags

**`tags = var.tags`** appears on the operator role and **`aws_lambda_capacity_provider`**.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — tags"
  tags = {
    Project = "demo"
    Owner   = "platform"
  }
```

2. **Apply** when the plan matches expectations. Note the **`capacity_provider_arn`** output — the next lab uses it.

## Overview

The [LMI stack lab](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/walkthrough/lmi/) provisions the capacity provider. This module builds the **function side**: the execution IAM role, a pre-created CloudWatch log group, and a **published** `aws_lambda_function` with `capacity_provider_config` pointing at the provider ARN.

Code blocks use **illustrative literals** so you can read the shape without tracing variables. Compare with **`examples/waf-loki`** (`lambda_managed_function_waf` in **`main.tf`**) when you apply.

<Aside type="caution">
  `capacity_provider_config` cannot be added or removed after the function is first created.
  **`per_execution_environment_max_concurrency`** is also **immutable** after first create. Plan
  both before the first `terraform apply` that creates the Lambda.
</Aside>

## Identity

The execution role uses a **`name_prefix`**. **`function_name`** and **`description`** go on **`aws_lambda_function`**. The **`capacity_provider_arn`** input is the output of the `lambda_managed_instance` module.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — identity-related fields"
# aws_iam_role.execution
name_prefix = "demo-exec-"

# aws_lambda_function.this
function_name         = "demo-fn"
description           = "Example Lambda Managed Instance function"
capacity_provider_arn = "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:111122223333:capacity-provider/demo-capacity"
```

Further reading: [IAM roles](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/operator-role/) (execution vs operator).

## Deployment artifact

The module does not build the deployment package; it passes through **`filename`** and **`source_code_hash`** on **`aws_lambda_function`**.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — deployment package"
  filename         = ".build/lambda.zip"
  source_code_hash = "dGhpcy1pcy1ub3QtYS1yZWFsLWhhc2gK"
```

## Function

LMI minimum memory is **2048 MB**. **`architectures`** must match the capacity provider's **`instance_requirements.architectures`**.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — runtime and sizing"
  handler       = "lambda_function.lambda_handler"
  runtime       = "python3.14"
  architectures = ["x86_64"]

  memory_size                    = 2048
  timeout                        = 30
  reserved_concurrent_executions = -1

  layers = null

  ephemeral_storage {
    size = 512
  }
```

Further reading: [Concurrency & runtimes](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/execution-environment/).

## Logging and log group

The module creates **`/aws/lambda/<function_name>`**, wires retention, and points **`logging_config.log_group`** at that group. With **`log_format = "JSON"`**, application and system log levels apply.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — CloudWatch log group"
  name              = "/aws/lambda/demo-fn"
  retention_in_days = 14
```

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — logging_config"
  logging_config {
    log_format            = "JSON"
    log_group             = "/aws/lambda/demo-fn"
    application_log_level = "INFO"
    system_log_level      = "WARN"
  }
```

The module offers a **`cloudwatch_log_group_prevent_destroy`** flag; when `true`, `lifecycle.prevent_destroy` is set so `terraform destroy` cannot delete the log group. Further reading: [Monitoring](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/monitoring/).

## Capacity provider link

**`capacity_provider_config`** is set on the function resource and references the capacity provider ARN. **`per_execution_environment_max_concurrency`** is part of this block and is **immutable** after the first create.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — function capacity_provider_config"
  capacity_provider_config {
    lambda_managed_instances_capacity_provider_config {
      capacity_provider_arn                     = "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:111122223333:capacity-provider/demo-capacity"
      per_execution_environment_max_concurrency = 10
    }
  }
```

## IAM (execution role extras)

**`AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole`** is always attached. Extra policies are separate attachments (one per ARN you need).

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — extra execution policy"
resource "aws_iam_role_policy_attachment" "execution_extra" {
  role       = "demo-exec-abc123"
  policy_arn = "arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess"
}
```

Further reading: [IAM roles](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/operator-role/).

## Tags

The same map is applied to the execution role and CloudWatch log group. **`aws_lambda_function`** in this module does not set **`tags`**.

```hcl title="Illustrative snippet — tags"
  tags = {
    Project = "demo"
    Owner   = "platform"
  }
```

## Invocation and triggers

**`lambda_managed_function`** only declares the function, execution role, and log group. In **`examples/waf-loki`**, **`main.tf`** adds what actually **invokes** the published function:

- **`aws_lambda_permission`** (`principal = s3.amazonaws.com`, **`source_arn`** = the log bucket) allows the bucket to invoke the function.
- **`aws_s3_bucket_notification`** sends **`s3:ObjectCreated:*`** to the function’s **qualified** ARN (published version). S3 requires **`function:name[:version]`** style ARN, not the API Gateway-style qualified invoke ARN (see the comment on that resource in the example).

<LambdaInvocationTriggersDiagram />

**On-demand**: You can still invoke from the Lambda console or with **`aws lambda invoke`**. The ingest handler expects **S3** event records for real work; a **`{}`** payload is enough for a **smoke** run (no `Records` → no object processing).

**Asynchronous path**: Creating an object in the configured bucket fires **`ObjectCreated`** → Lambda. Scope is controlled by **`waf_logs_prefix`** (empty = entire bucket) and **`waf_logs_object_suffix`** (default **`.gz`**; empty omits the suffix filter — useful for test uploads, risky on shared buckets). Use **`terraform output waf_logs_bucket`** to confirm the bucket name.

**Optional upstream**: If **`web_acl_arn`** is set, **`aws_wafv2_web_acl_logging_configuration`** writes WAF logs into the **same** bucket, so delivery can become WAF → S3 → notification → Lambda. Destroy removes that logging configuration, not the Web ACL itself, if the ACL pre-existed.

## Review the plan

<Steps>

1. **From `examples/waf-loki`,** run **`terraform plan`**. <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Expect the execution role and its attachments, the CloudWatch log group, the Lambda function with **`publish = true`** and **`capacity_provider_config`**, plus the example’s **`aws_lambda_permission`** and **`aws_s3_bucket_notification`** for the WAF ingest function.

2. **Compare** your intended **`memory_size`**, **`per_execution_environment_max_concurrency`**, and capacity provider settings with [Publishing & activation](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/version-publishing/) before **`terraform apply`**.

3. **Apply** when the plan matches expectations. First publish can take several minutes while capacity becomes active.

</Steps>

## After apply

Confirm the **published** function on the capacity provider and its **CloudWatch** log group. You can do that without opening **Grafana** or proving **Loki** ingest — those matter for the full observability demo, not for “Lambda on LMI responds.”

Work from **`examples/waf-loki`** (same directory and AWS credentials **region** as **`terraform apply`**).

### Read Terraform outputs

**`waf_function_name`**, **`waf_function_version`**, **`waf_log_group_name`**, and **`waf_logs_bucket`** (bucket wired to the notification).

```bash frame="terminal" title="Terraform outputs"
terraform output waf_function_name
terraform output waf_function_version
terraform output waf_log_group_name
terraform output waf_logs_bucket
```

### Console and logs

In **Lambda**, open the function and confirm a **numbered** published version matches **`terraform output`** (the module sets **`publish = true`**; seeing **`$LATEST`** as well is normal). In **CloudWatch → Log groups**, open **`waf_log_group_name`** and confirm a log stream appears after you invoke (next step).

### CLI smoke check

The AWS CLI **must** have a region for Lambda; without it, commands fail until you specify one. Use the **same** region as **`var.aws_region`** in this workspace’s **`terraform.tfvars`** (the snippet uses **`ap-southeast-2`** — change it if your stack is elsewhere). For a persistent default, run **`aws configure`** or **`aws configure set region <your-region>`**.

```bash frame="terminal" title="get-function and invoke (published version)"
export AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2

NAME=$(terraform output -raw waf_function_name)
VER=$(terraform output -raw waf_function_version)
aws lambda get-function --region "$AWS_REGION" --function-name "$NAME" --qualifier "$VER" \
  --query 'Configuration.{State:State,LastUpdateStatus:LastUpdateStatus,Version:Version}' \
  --output table
aws lambda invoke --region "$AWS_REGION" --function-name "$NAME" --qualifier "$VER" \
  --cli-binary-format raw-in-base64-out --payload '{}' /tmp/lmi-smoke.json
cat /tmp/lmi-smoke.json
```

An empty **`{}`** payload does not simulate **S3** event records; it is only a quick check that the function runs on **LMI**. When it succeeds, **`get-function`** shows **`State: Active`**, **`LastUpdateStatus: Successful`**, and a **`Version`** that matches your **`terraform output`**. **`invoke`** prints something like the following (your version number may differ):

```text frame="terminal" title="Example CLI output after a successful smoke invoke"
-------------------------------------------
|               GetFunction               |
+-------------------+---------+-----------+
| LastUpdateStatus  |  State  |  Version  |
+-------------------+---------+-----------+
|  Successful       |  Active |  4        |
+-------------------+---------+-----------+

{
    "StatusCode": 200,
    "ExecutedVersion": "4"
}
```

**`StatusCode: 200`** means the invocation completed without a Lambda service error; **`ExecutedVersion`** should match the published **`--qualifier`**. The response body is written to **`/tmp/lmi-smoke.json`** (often empty or minimal for this handler with no **`Records`**).

### Optional S3 notification path

To exercise **`aws_s3_bucket_notification`**, create an object in **`waf_logs_bucket`** whose key matches **`waf_logs_prefix`** and **`waf_logs_object_suffix`** (see [Invocation and triggers](#invocation-and-triggers)). Confirm a new invocation and log lines. A full **S3 → Lambda → Loki** check is optional and assumes the observability host and **`LOKI_URL`** are healthy.

### Cleanup

From **`examples/waf-loki`**, run **`terraform destroy`** in the same workspace you used for apply.

The **WAF log bucket** is an **existing** bucket (referenced by **`waf_logs_bucket_name`**); this stack does **not** delete the bucket or its objects. Empty or delete the bucket yourself if you created it only for the lab. If **`web_acl_arn`** was set, destroy removes the **logging configuration** resource, not the Web ACL.

For metrics, filters, and alarms, see **[Monitoring](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/monitoring/)**; for how publishes relate to capacity, see **[Publishing & activation](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/version-publishing/)**.

That finishes the **current** lab sequence: **VPC and networking**, **LMI stack**, then **Lambda Managed Function**. Concept pages under **[LMI fundamentals](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/)** remain the place for placement, scaling, and trade-offs. Further procedural labs will appear in the sidebar when those pages are enabled.

Deploy the walkthrough VPC stack with Terraform — subnets, NAT, routes, and the security group that capacity provider ENIs use.

import { Steps, Aside, FileTree, Badge, CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import Checklist from "@/components/Checklist.astro";
import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro";
import NetworkingVpcIpv4TrafficDiagram from "@/components/NetworkingVpcIpv4TrafficDiagram.astro";

<Aside type="note">
  Terraform code:
  [terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance).
  This lab uses
  **[examples/waf-loki](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance/tree/main/examples/waf-loki)**
  and the shared
  **[modules/vpc](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance/tree/main/modules/vpc)**
  module (the same networking building block described in that repository’s own docs).
</Aside>

<Checklist
  id="walkthrough-networking"
  items={[
    "Companion repo cloned and examples/waf-loki reachable",
    "aws_region and availability_zones aligned in tfvars or variables",
    "terraform init and terraform plan run without errors",
    "terraform apply completed (or you understand what the plan will create)",
    "Optional: VPC and subnets visible in the EC2 console",
  ]}
/>

## Overview

Lambda Managed Instances run managed <Tooltip term="ec2" /> capacity in **your** <Tooltip term="vpc" />. In this repository, **`modules/vpc`** builds a small, repeatable layout: **public** <Tooltip term="subnet" />s for an <Tooltip term="igw" /> and a <Tooltip term="nat" /> gateway, **private** subnets for workloads, and route tables so private IPv4 egress goes through NAT. The walkthrough example also defines **`aws_security_group.lmi`**: a <Tooltip term="sg" /> attached to the <Tooltip term="capacity-provider" /> so ENIs can reach the Lambda control plane and CloudWatch Logs (egress allowed in the example).

For **why** you might choose public subnets, endpoints, or NAT in production, read [VPC connectivity for Lambda Managed Instances](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/networking/). For **placement** (subnets and security groups as the provider boundary), see [Placement & capacity](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/capacity-providers/).

<Aside type="caution">
  `capacity_provider_config` cannot be added or removed after the function is first created. Plan
  your function configuration before the first `terraform apply` that creates the Lambda. See
  [Publishing &
  activation](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/version-publishing/).
</Aside>

## Repository layout

<FileTree>
  - examples/waf-loki/ - **main.tf** - **variables.tf** - function/ - … - modules/vpc/ - **main.tf**
  - **variables.tf** - …
</FileTree>

## Traffic path

AWS resources and traffic flow for the default layout **`modules/vpc`** provisions (used by **examples/waf-loki**):

<NetworkingVpcIpv4TrafficDiagram />

Private subnets have no path from the internet **inbound** by default; outbound IPv4 uses the NAT gateway in a public subnet. The capacity provider places ENIs in the **private** subnets you pass from this VPC.

<Steps>

1. **Finish prerequisites.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Complete [Git](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/git/) (clone the Terraform repo), [Terraform](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/terraform/), and [What else](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/what-else/) — especially **one region** and matching **Availability Zones** for every resource.

2. **Open the walkthrough example directory.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   cd terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance/examples/waf-loki
   ```

3. **Configure region and networks (optional).**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars
   ```

   Edit `terraform.tfvars` or rely on defaults. Keep `aws_region` and `availability_zones` consistent — for example `ap-southeast-2` with `ap-southeast-2a`, `ap-southeast-2b`, and `ap-southeast-2c`. The example defaults to **`10.0.0.0/16`**, with public subnets **`10.0.1.0/24`**, **`10.0.2.0/24`**, **`10.0.3.0/24`** and private subnets **`10.0.101.0/24`**, **`10.0.102.0/24`**, **`10.0.103.0/24`** (one public/private pair per AZ). If you run more than one stack in the same account, choose non-overlapping `vpc_cidr` and subnet lists. Confirm [Lambda Managed Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances.html) is available in your chosen region.

4. **Install providers and review the plan.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   terraform init
   terraform plan
   ```

   In the plan, expect a <Tooltip term="vpc" />, **three public and three private subnets** across three Availability Zones, an internet gateway, **one NAT gateway** (default `single_nat_gateway` in `modules/vpc`), route tables (public `0.0.0.0/0` → IGW; private → NAT), the **LMI security group** (egress-only to `0.0.0.0/0` in the example), plus IAM, CloudWatch log group, capacity provider, and Lambda from `modules/lambda_managed_instance`.

5. **Apply.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   terraform apply
   ```

   First apply can take several minutes while capacity and the published function version become active.

6. **Sanity-check in the console (optional).**

   In **VPC** and **EC2 → Subnets**, confirm CIDRs and route tables match the plan. After traffic exists, managed instances may show service tags such as `aws:lambda:capacity-provider` — those are applied by AWS, not by your `tags` block in Terraform.

</Steps>

Create or use an AWS account, secure the root user, and configure day-to-day access before anything else.

import { Steps, Aside, Badge } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import Checklist from "@/components/Checklist.astro";
import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro";

<Checklist
  id="prereq-aws-account"
  items={[
    "AWS account created or confirmed available",
    "MFA enabled on the root user",
    "IAM user or IAM Identity Center access created for day-to-day use",
    "Billing alerts enabled and default region chosen",
  ]}
/>

This walkthrough creates a <Tooltip term="vpc" />, <Tooltip term="capacity-provider" />, <Tooltip term="lambda" /> functions, and related <Tooltip term="iam" /> resources. You need an <Tooltip term="aws" /> account with permissions to provision all of these. Root access is only needed for initial setup — you'll create a dedicated user (or use IAM Identity Center) for all day-to-day work.

<Steps>

1. **Create or sign in to an AWS account.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   If you don't have an account yet, sign up at [aws.amazon.com](https://aws.amazon.com). Account creation is free — you only pay for the resources you create.

   If you already have an account, sign in as the root user (or an admin) to complete the steps on this page.

2. **Enable MFA on the root user.** <Badge text="Recommended" variant="note" size="small" />

   The root user has unrestricted access to everything in the account. Protect it before doing anything else.

   In the AWS Console:
   - Click your account name (top right) → **Security credentials**
   - Under **Multi-factor authentication (MFA)**, choose **Assign MFA device**
   - Choose **Authenticator app** (e.g. Google Authenticator, 1Password, Authy) or a hardware key
   - Follow the prompts to scan the QR code and enter two consecutive OTP codes to confirm

   After MFA is set up, sign out and sign back in to verify it works.

3. **Create a dedicated IAM user or use IAM Identity Center for day-to-day work.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Avoid running Terraform or the AWS CLI as the root user. Create a dedicated identity instead:

   <Aside type="tip">
     If you prefer **short-lived credentials** and centralised users, set up **IAM Identity Center**
     and skip creating long-lived IAM user access keys. See the [IAM Identity
     Center](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/iam-identity-center/)
     page.
   </Aside>

   **IAM user path (simpler, access keys):**
   - In the Console: IAM → Users → **Create user**
   - Set a user name (e.g. `lmi-admin`)
   - Attach a policy — for the walkthrough use **AdministratorAccess** (or scope it to VPC, Lambda, IAM, EC2, S3, and related services you plan to use)
   - Under **Security credentials**: Create access key → choose **CLI** use case → note the Access Key ID and Secret Access Key

   You will enter these into `aws configure` in the [AWS CLI](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-cli/) step.

4. **Enable billing alerts.** <Badge text="Recommended" variant="note" size="small" />

   NAT gateways, EC2 capacity for <Tooltip term="managed-instances" />, and other resources incur cost. Set up a billing alert so you're notified if spend exceeds a threshold.

   In the Console:
   - Billing and Cost Management → **Budgets** → Create a budget
   - Choose **Cost budget**, set a monthly threshold (e.g. $50 USD), and add an email alert

5. **Choose and note your AWS region.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Pick one region for the entire walkthrough. This walkthrough uses `ap-southeast-2` (AWS Asia Pacific — New Zealand). All CLI commands and Terraform will use this region as the default.

   Note it down — you'll enter it during `aws configure` and in your Terraform variables.

   <Aside type="note">
     Not all services or EC2 instance types are available in every region. If you hit an "instance
     type not supported" error, try a different instance type within the same region.
   </Aside>

</Steps>

## Next step

Continue to [IAM Identity Center](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/iam-identity-center/) to set up centralised access and short-lived credentials, or skip ahead to [AWS CLI](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-cli/) if you are using an IAM user with access keys only.
Install the AWS CLI and configure credentials - either IAM user access keys or IAM Identity Center SSO.

import { Steps, Aside, Tabs, TabItem, Badge } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import Checklist from "@/components/Checklist.astro";
import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro";

<Checklist
  id="prereq-aws-cli"
  items={[
    "AWS CLI v2 installed",
    "Credentials configured (IAM user or SSO)",
    "aws sts get-caller-identity returns expected account and identity",
  ]}
/>

The AWS CLI is how you authenticate to <Tooltip term="aws" /> from your machine. Terraform uses the same credential chain to create resources. Getting this right first avoids authentication errors in every subsequent step.

<Steps>

1. **Install the AWS CLI.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   <Tabs syncKey="os">
     <TabItem label="macOS">

     ```bash frame="terminal" title="Install via Homebrew"
     brew install awscli
     ```

     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="Linux">

     If needed, install `unzip` and `curl` first (for example `sudo apt install -y unzip curl` or `sudo dnf install -y unzip curl`).

     ```bash frame="terminal" title="Install via curl"
     curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-x86_64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
     unzip awscliv2.zip
     sudo ./aws/install --update
     ```

     For ARM (for example Graviton or Raspberry Pi):

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     curl "https://awscli.amazonaws.com/awscli-exe-linux-aarch64.zip" -o "awscliv2.zip"
     unzip awscliv2.zip
     sudo ./aws/install --update
     ```

     </TabItem>
   </Tabs>

   Verify the installation:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   aws --version
   ```

   You should see `aws-cli/2.x.x` - version 2 is required.

2. **Configure AWS access.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Choose the path that matches how you set up access in the previous steps.

   <Tabs syncKey="credential-type">
     <TabItem label="IAM user (access keys)">

     You need the Access Key ID and Secret Access Key you created in the [AWS Account](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-account/) step.

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     aws configure
     ```

     Enter the following when prompted:
     - **AWS Access Key ID** - the key ID (starts with `AKIA...`)
     - **AWS Secret Access Key** - the secret
     - **Default region name** - the region you chose (for example `ap-southeast-2`)
     - **Default output format** - `json`

     This writes credentials to `~/.aws/credentials` and config to `~/.aws/config`.

     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="IAM Identity Center (SSO)">

     If you completed the [IAM Identity Center](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/iam-identity-center/) step, your profile is already configured. Log in:

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     aws sso login --profile lmi-walkthrough
     ```

     To avoid typing `--profile` on every command, set the profile as your shell default:

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     export AWS_PROFILE=lmi-walkthrough
     ```

     Add the export to `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc` to persist it across sessions.

     SSO sessions expire (typically after 1-8 hours depending on your permission set). Re-run `aws sso login` when the session expires.

     </TabItem>
   </Tabs>

3. **Verify access.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   aws sts get-caller-identity
   ```

   Expected output:

   ```json
   {
     "UserId": "AIDA...",
     "Account": "123456789012",
     "Arn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/lmi-admin"
   }
   ```

   For IAM Identity Center the ARN will contain `assumed-role` instead of `user`. Either is correct as long as the account ID matches your account.

   <Aside type="caution">
     If the command returns an error like `Unable to locate credentials`, re-run `aws configure`
     (IAM user) or `aws sso login` (SSO) and try again.
   </Aside>

4. **Confirm the default region.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   aws configure get region
   ```

   The output should match the region you chose. If it is blank or wrong, set it:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   aws configure set region ap-southeast-2
   ```

   Replace `ap-southeast-2` with your region if different.

</Steps>

Enable IAM Identity Center, create a user and permission set, and configure the AWS CLI to use short-lived SSO credentials.

import { Steps, Aside, Badge } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import Checklist from "@/components/Checklist.astro";
import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro";

<Checklist
  id="prereq-iam-identity-center"
  items={[
    "IAM Identity Center enabled in the AWS Console",
    "User (and optional group) created in the Identity Center directory",
    "Permission set assigned to the user for the target account",
    "AWS CLI profile configured for SSO login",
  ]}
/>

<Tooltip term="iam-identity-center" label="IAM Identity Center" /> (formerly
<Tooltip term="sso" label="AWS SSO" />) gives you centralised user management, single sign-on, and
short-lived credentials instead of long-lived access keys tied to an IAM user.

Why use it:

- Avoids long-lived IAM user access keys and passwords.
- Built-in MFA and optional federated login.
- Free for internal users; scalable and audit-friendly.
- Fits teams and workflows that already standardise on <Tooltip term="oidc" label="OIDC" /> and IAM Identity Center for temporary AWS credentials.

You need one-time root (or admin) access to enable IAM Identity Center; MFA on the root user is recommended (see [AWS Account](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-account/)).

<Aside type="tip">
  If you prefer a plain IAM user with access keys, you can skip this page and continue with [AWS
  CLI](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-cli/).
</Aside>

<Steps>

1.  **Enable IAM Identity Center.** <Badge text="Optional" variant="note" size="small" />

    In the AWS Console, search for **IAM Identity Center** (or find it under Security, Identity and Compliance).
    - Click **Enable**
    - On the identity source prompt, keep the default: **Identity Center directory** (unless you already have an external IdP such as Okta or Azure AD; connect it here if so)
    - Choose the same region you selected in the [AWS Account](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-account/) step. IAM Identity Center is a regional service; the region you enable it in is where the service runs

    <Aside type="note">
      If you see "IAM Identity Center is already enabled", someone (or a prior setup) has already
      done this. Click through to check the existing configuration before creating new users.
    </Aside>

2.  **Create a user in the Identity Center directory.**

    In IAM Identity Center:
    - Left nav: **Users** then **Add user**
    - Enter a username and email address (for example name `Admin` and your email)
    - Fill in first name, last name
    - Leave **Send an email to the user with password setup instructions** checked; you will receive an activation email
    - Click **Add user**

    Check your email and complete the account activation (set a password and configure MFA for the Identity Center user too).

3.  **Create a group (optional but recommended).**

    Groups let you assign permission sets to multiple users at once.
    - Left nav: **Groups** then **Create group**
    - Name it (for example `Admins` or `LMI-Walkthrough`)
    - Add your user to the group

4.  **Create a permission set.**

    A permission set defines what the user (or group) can do in an account.
    - Left nav: **Permission sets** then **Create permission set**
    - Choose **Predefined permission set** then **AdministratorAccess** (for the walkthrough; scope it down for production use)
    - Accept defaults for session duration (1 hour is fine; extend to 8 hours if your Terraform runs take longer)
    - Name it (for example `AdministratorAccess`) and create it

5.  **Assign the user (or group) to the AWS account.**
    - Left nav: **AWS accounts** then select your account from the list
    - Click **Assign users or groups**
    - Choose your user (or group), then Next
    - Choose the permission set you just created, then Next, then Submit

    Wait a moment for the assignment to apply.

6.  **Verify console access (optional but recommended).**

    In IAM Identity Center then **Settings**, copy the **Access portal URL** (for example `https://d-xxxxxxxxxx.awsapps.com/start`). Open it in a browser, sign in with the Identity Center user you created, and click **Management Console** for your account to confirm access.

7.  **Configure the AWS CLI to use IAM Identity Center.** <Badge text="Required if using SSO" variant="tip" size="small" />

    Requires **AWS CLI v2** (see [AWS CLI](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-cli/) to install). After the CLI is installed, run:

    ```bash frame="terminal"
    aws configure sso
    ```

    You will be asked for:
    - **SSO session name** - any name (for example `my-sso`)
    - **SSO start URL** - find this in IAM Identity Center then Settings (looks like `https://d-xxxxxxxxxx.awsapps.com/start`)
    - **SSO region** - the region where you enabled IAM Identity Center (for example `ap-southeast-2`)
    - **SSO registration scopes** - press Enter to accept the default (`sso:account:access`)
    - A browser opens for you to sign in and approve access
    - Back in the terminal: choose the account and permission set, then set a profile name (for example `lmi-walkthrough`)
    - **Default client Region** - use the same region as your SSO region (for example `ap-southeast-2`)
    - Default output format: `json`

8.  **Log in and verify.**

    ```bash frame="terminal"
    aws sso login --profile lmi-walkthrough
    aws sts get-caller-identity --profile lmi-walkthrough
    ```

    The output should show your account ID and a role ARN from IAM Identity Center (not an IAM user ARN).

        <Aside type="tip">

    Set the profile as default so you do not need `--profile` on every command:

    ```bash frame="terminal"
    export AWS_PROFILE=lmi-walkthrough
    aws sts get-caller-identity
    ```

    Add the export to your shell profile (`~/.bashrc`, `~/.zshrc`) to persist it.

        </Aside>

        <Aside type="note" title="Session expiry">

    SSO sessions typically expire after 8-12 hours. When credentials expire, run `aws sso login --profile lmi-walkthrough` again. To check when the session expires:

    ```bash frame="terminal"
    jq -r 'select(.startUrl != null) | .expiresAt' ~/.aws/sso/cache/*.json
    ```

        </Aside>

    <Aside type="note" title="Remove the profile later">
      To remove the profile, delete the `[profile lmi-walkthrough]` block from your AWS config file
      (for example `$HOME/.aws/config`) and any related entries in `$HOME/.aws/credentials` if
      present.
    </Aside>

</Steps>

## Optional: harden the setup

- **MFA in IAM Identity Center** - In IAM Identity Center then **Settings** then **Multi-factor authentication**, require MFA for all users.
- **CloudTrail** - In CloudTrail, create a trail for management events (free tier applies).
- **Root access keys** - If any root access keys exist, remove them in IAM. Use the root account only for rare account-level tasks.

Git is used to clone the Terraform infrastructure repo used by this walkthrough. Install it, set your identity, and clone the infra repo before running any Terraform from the repo.

<Steps>

1. **Install Git.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   <Tabs syncKey="os">
     <TabItem label="macOS">

     macOS ships with a system Git, but Homebrew's version is kept up to date:

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     brew install git
     ```

     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="Linux">

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y git
     ```

     </TabItem>
   </Tabs>

   Verify:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   git --version
   ```

2. **Configure your identity.**

   Git requires a name and email for commits. This matters if you push any changes later (for example to your own fork of the Terraform repo).

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   git config --global user.name "Your Name"
   git config --global user.email "you@example.com"
   ```

   Confirm the settings:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   git config --global --list
   ```

3. **Clone the Terraform infrastructure repo.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   The Terraform code for this walkthrough lives in a separate repository. Clone it into your workspace directory.

   <Aside type="note">
     Repository:
     [terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance).
     This walkthrough uses **`examples/waf-loki`** and shared modules under **`modules/`** (see
     that repository’s own documentation for the full tree).
   </Aside>

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   cd ~/workspace
   git clone https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance.git
   cd terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance
   ```

   Confirm the clone worked:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   ls
   ```

   You should see `README.md`, `modules/`, `examples/waf-loki/`, and other Terraform files as described in that repository.

4. **Optional: use SSH instead of HTTPS.**

   If you have an SSH key configured with GitHub, use SSH to avoid entering your credentials on every `git pull`:

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   git clone git@github.com:jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance.git
   ```

   To set up an SSH key: [GitHub docs - generating a new SSH key](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent).

5. **Verify the remote.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   git remote -v
   ```

   Expected output:

   ```text
   origin  https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance.git (fetch)
   origin  https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance.git (push)
   ```

</Steps>

import { Steps, Aside, Tabs, TabItem, Badge } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import Checklist from "@/components/Checklist.astro";
import Tooltip from "@/components/Tooltip.astro";

<Checklist
  id="prereq-terraform"
  items={[
    "Terraform >= 1.5 installed",

  ]}
/>

All infrastructure in this walkthrough - VPC, subnets, <Tooltip term="capacity-provider" />, <Tooltip term="lambda" /> functions, <Tooltip term="iam" /> roles - is created and destroyed with <Tooltip term="terraform" label="Terraform" />. Nothing is created manually in the console (except one-off checks). Using Terraform means:

- Every change is reviewed before it is applied
- The full environment can be torn down with one command (`terraform destroy`)
- State tracks what has been created so incremental updates are safe

This site expects the **HashiCorp AWS provider `~> 6.0`** so resources such as `aws_lambda_capacity_provider` and `capacity_provider_config` on `aws_lambda_function` are available. The Terraform code lives in a **separate repository** ([terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance](https://github.com/jajera/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance)). You will clone it in the [Git](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/git/) step.

<Steps>

1. **Install Terraform.** <Badge text="Required" variant="tip" size="small" />

   Use Terraform 1.5 or later. The walkthrough uses features from 1.5+.

   <Tabs syncKey="os">
     <TabItem label="macOS">

     ```bash frame="terminal" title="Install via Homebrew (HashiCorp tap)"
     brew tap hashicorp/tap
     brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform
     ```

     To upgrade later:

     ```bash frame="terminal"
     brew upgrade hashicorp/tap/terraform
     ```

     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="Debian/Ubuntu">

     Add the HashiCorp APT repository and install. If needed, install dependencies first: `curl`, `gnupg`, `software-properties-common`.

     ```bash frame="terminal" title="Add HashiCorp repo (Debian/Ubuntu)"
     sudo apt update
     sudo apt install -y curl gnupg software-properties-common
     curl -fsSL https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg \
       | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg
     echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/hashicorp-archive-keyring.gpg] \
       https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" \
       | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list
     sudo apt update
     sudo apt install -y terraform
     ```

     </TabItem>
     <TabItem label="RHEL / Fedora (dnf)">

     Add the HashiCorp repository and install. If needed, install `curl` and `gnupg2` first (for example `sudo dnf install -y curl gnupg2`).

     ```bash frame="terminal" title="Add HashiCorp repo (RHEL/Fedora)"
     sudo dnf config-manager --add-repo https://rpm.releases.hashicorp.com/RHEL/hashicorp.repo
     sudo dnf install -y terraform
     ```

     </TabItem>
   </Tabs>

2. **Verify the installation.**

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   terraform version
   ```

   Expected output:

   ```text
   Terraform v1.x.x
   on linux_amd64
   ```

   The version must be 1.5 or higher. If you have an older version installed via a different method, uninstall it first.

3. **Create a workspace directory.**

   Create a dedicated directory where you will clone and run Terraform. This keeps the walkthrough files separate from other projects.

   ```bash frame="terminal"
   mkdir -p ~/workspace
   cd ~/workspace
   ```

   You will clone the Terraform repo into this directory in the [Git](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/git/) step.

4. **Understand the state setup.**

   <Aside type="note" title="Local state (default)">

   This walkthrough uses **local state** - Terraform writes a `terraform.tfstate` file in the working directory. That is fine for a solo walkthrough on your own machine.

   For production use or shared team access, move to **remote state** (for example S3 + DynamoDB backend) so state is stored safely and multiple people or CI can apply changes without conflicts. The [What else](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/what-else/) page has notes on this.

   </Aside>

</Steps>

Where LMI runs in your account — VPC wiring, instance requirements, provider controls, and what AWS still operates for you.

import { Aside, CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import CapacityProviderResponsibilityBoundaryDiagram from "@/components/CapacityProviderResponsibilityBoundaryDiagram.astro";

A **capacity provider** is the placement boundary for LMI. You define **where** compute runs (VPC, subnets, security groups) and the **instance requirements** Lambda can use (for example `x86_64` or `arm64`). A function uses that provider through **`capacity_provider_config`** on `aws_lambda_function`.

<Aside type="caution">
  `capacity_provider_config` cannot be added or removed after the function is first created. Decide
  whether the function runs on LMI before you create that function. See [Publishing &
  activation](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/version-publishing/).
</Aside>

## Placement model

| Element                   | What you decide                                      | Why it matters                                         |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| **VPC placement**         | Subnets and security groups in your account          | Defines network reachability and boundaries            |
| **Instance requirements** | Architecture and performance constraints             | Lets Lambda pick matching current-generation instances |
| **Scaling config**        | `scaling_mode`, `max_vcpu_count`, and related limits | Caps fleet growth for this provider                    |

You manage provider configuration; Lambda manages how that configuration is realized as running capacity.

## Supported instance families

| Family                    | x86_64 (Intel & AMD) | arm64 (Graviton) |
| ------------------------- | -------------------- | ---------------- |
| **C** (compute optimized) | C5, C6, C7           | C6G, C7G, C8G    |
| **M** (general purpose)   | M5, M6, M7           | M6G, M7G, M8G    |
| **R** (memory optimized)  | R5, R6, R7           | R6G, R7G, R8G    |

- Use **`.large` or larger** sizes within these families.
- **Default:** Lambda selects a suitable current-generation type from the allowed set for your architecture and function configuration.
- **Override:** narrow the pool with **allowed** or **excluded** instance types on the capacity provider (only one of those lists applies).

<Aside type="note">
  **Further reading:** SKU-level availability is **region-specific**. On [AWS Lambda
  pricing](https://aws.amazon.com/lambda/pricing/), choose your **Region**, open **Lambda Managed
  Instances**, and use the **Management Fees** section as the enumerated list. For API fields, see
  [Capacity
  providers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-capacity-providers.html);
  for how overrides interact with scaling, see [Scaling — Instance type
  selection](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-scaling.html).
</Aside>

## Fully managed (what AWS operates)

<CapacityProviderResponsibilityBoundaryDiagram />

With LMI, you do not run the fleet like normal EC2 operations. You configure placement and limits; Lambda runs lifecycle, patching, routing, and managed scaling behavior for the provider fleet.

For quota-sensitive and API-field details, use [Lambda Managed Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances.html) as the product reference.

How default Lambda and LMI differ in execution sandboxes — concurrency models, the five supported language floors, Shared Lambda infrastructure versus Nitro, and IO-bound workloads.

import { Aside, CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";
import ExecutionEnvironmentDefaultVsLmiDiagram from "@/components/ExecutionEnvironmentDefaultVsLmiDiagram.astro";

The fundamental difference is **where and how** execution environments run and how many invocations each environment can handle at once.

<ExecutionEnvironmentDefaultVsLmiDiagram />

<Aside type="note">
  For quota- and region-sensitive behaviour, use [Lambda Managed
  Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances.html) as the
  product reference.
</Aside>

## Concurrency model

- **Lambda (default):** **single concurrency** — at most **one invocation** per execution environment at a time.
- **LMI:** **multi-concurrent invocations** — one environment can process **multiple** invocations simultaneously, improving utilisation of underlying EC2 — especially for **IO-heavy** apps (AWS calls out web services and batch-style work).

Multi-concurrency changes how you think about **thread safety**, **global state**, and **context isolation** ([Concurrency model](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances.html)).

## Supported runtimes

LMI does not support every default-Lambda runtime. The supported **language floors** and **multi-concurrency behaviour** are defined on the product pages; the table below matches the [Supported languages](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-runtimes.html#supported-languages) list as of that document.

| Language    | Minimum (per AWS)                       | Example `runtime` values                                 | Multi-concurrency (per AWS)                                                    |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **Java**    | Java 21 and later                       | `java21`, … (newer managed identifiers as AWS adds them) | One process, OS threads — handler must be thread-safe                          |
| **Python**  | Python 3.13 and later                   | `python3.14`, `python3.13`, …                            | One process **per concurrent request** — watch shared resources such as `/tmp` |
| **Node.js** | Node.js 22 and later                    | `nodejs22.x`, `nodejs24.x`, …                            | Worker threads + async; safe shared state                                      |
| **.NET**    | .NET 8 and later                        | `dotnet8`, …                                             | Tasks / async; safe shared state                                               |
| **Rust**    | OS-only **`provided.al2023` and later** | `provided.al2023`, …                                     | One process, Tokio tasks — handler `Clone` + `Send`                            |

Exact `runtime` strings, region availability, and new language versions live in the language topics and API reference. Use the [LMI runtimes hub](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-runtimes.html) (and **Next steps** there) before you pin a `runtime` value in config.

<Aside type="caution">
  **Multi-concurrency changes isolation semantics versus default Lambda.** Whether invocations share
  a process, threads, or separate processes depends on the **runtime** (see the table above and the
  language topics linked from the hub). Treat global state, caches, connections, and `/tmp`
  accordingly before you migrate or raise concurrency.
</Aside>

Before creating any infrastructure, take five minutes to confirm a few things that affect every step that follows. A wrong region or forgotten teardown can cause confusing errors or unexpected charges.

## Region

Use **one AWS region** for the entire walkthrough. VPC, subnets, <Tooltip term="capacity-provider" />, <Tooltip term="lambda" /> functions, and IAM roles should stay consistent with that region. Mixing regions causes routing issues and authentication failures.

**How to set the default region:**

For IAM user credentials (`aws configure`):

```bash frame="terminal"
aws configure set region ap-southeast-2
```

For SSO (set it in your profile config, e.g. `~/.aws/config`):

```ini title="~/.aws/config"
[profile lmi-walkthrough]
sso_start_url = https://d-xxxxxxxxxx.awsapps.com/start
sso_region = ap-southeast-2
sso_account_id = 123456789012
sso_role_name = AdministratorAccess
region = ap-southeast-2
output = json
```

Terraform reads the region from the AWS provider config or from the `AWS_REGION` environment variable:

```bash frame="terminal"
export AWS_REGION=ap-southeast-2
```

Or set it in `terraform.tfvars` in the Terraform repo (the repo's README explains how).

**This walkthrough uses:** `ap-southeast-2` (AWS Asia Pacific — New Zealand).

<Aside type="note">
  Not all EC2 instance types are available in every region. If you hit an "instance type not
  supported" error, try a different instance type within `ap-southeast-2`.
</Aside>

## Terraform state

This walkthrough uses **local state** — Terraform writes `terraform.tfstate` to the directory where you run commands. This is the simplest setup and works fine for a solo walkthrough.

**Why local state is fine here:**

- You're the only person running Terraform
- The state file lives in the cloned repo directory on your machine
- No S3 bucket or DynamoDB table to set up

**When to move to remote state (later):**

If you continue using this setup for real workloads, or if multiple people or CI need to run Terraform, switch to a remote backend. A common pattern for AWS:

```hcl title="backend.tf (not needed now)"
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-terraform-state"
    key            = "lmi/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "ap-southeast-2"
    dynamodb_table = "terraform-state-lock"
  }
}
```

This is **not required** for the walkthrough. You can adopt remote state when you productionise.

## Cost

The resources created in this walkthrough are **not free tier** in general. Approximate cost while running depends on how much EC2 capacity you provision for <Tooltip term="managed-instances" />, whether you use NAT gateways, and how often Lambda runs. Order-of-magnitude examples:

| Resource                     | Approximate cost                                 |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------ |
| NAT Gateway                  | ~$0.045 / hour + data transfer                   |
| EC2 (capacity provider pool) | Depends on instance type and count               |
| Lambda                       | Usage-based (often small relative to NAT/EC2)    |
| **Total**                    | **Highly variable** — use budgets and dashboards |

<Aside type="caution" title="Cost and cleanup">
  Always run `terraform destroy` when you finish a session. NAT gateways, EC2 instances, and other billable resources continue to charge while they exist — even if you're not invoking Lambda. Check the [AWS Billing console](https://console.aws.amazon.com/billing/) if unsure what's running.

```bash frame="terminal" title="Tear down (run from the Terraform root or module path in your clone)"
cd ~/workspace/terraform-aws-lambda-managed-instance
terraform destroy
```

Use the directory and workspace layout described in the Terraform repo's README if it differs.

</Aside>

To avoid surprise charges:

- Set a billing alert (covered in the [AWS Account](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/foundations/prerequisites/aws-account/) step)
- Run `terraform destroy` at the end of each session
- Check the EC2 and Lambda dashboards in the Console after destroying to confirm nothing is left running

CloudWatch metrics for LMI at capacity-provider and execution-environment levels — utilisation, capacity, concurrency, and how to use them for sizing.

import { CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

LMI publishes **CloudWatch** metrics so you can watch **fleet utilisation**, **remaining capacity**, and **per-function execution environment** behaviour. Metrics help right-size memory, interpret scaling, and spot concurrency pressure.

For full metric lists, dimensions, and console navigation, use [Monitoring Lambda Managed Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-monitoring.html) as the product reference.

## Two metric scopes

| Scope                     | What it answers                                                                     | Example dimensions (per AWS)                             |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Capacity provider**     | How hard the **managed fleet** is working and how much headroom remains             | **CapacityProviderName**, **InstanceType**               |
| **Execution environment** | How a **specific function version** uses CPU, memory, and concurrency on that fleet | **CapacityProviderName**, **FunctionName**, **Resource** |

For LMI, the **Resource** dimension on execution-environment metrics targets **function versions** — format **`<FunctionName>:<FunctionVersion>`** (not aliases-only semantics in the same way).

## Highlights (what to watch)

### Capacity provider (fleet) level

| Metric (names per AWS)                    | Use                                                   |
| ----------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| **CPUUtilization**, **MemoryUtilization** | Overall pressure across instances behind the provider |
| **vCPUAvailable**, **MemoryAvailable**    | Headroom still allocatable to environments            |
| **vCPUAllocated**, **MemoryAllocated**    | What is already committed to execution environments   |

### Execution environment (function version) level

| Metric (names per AWS)                                                            | Use                                                  |
| --------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| **ExecutionEnvironmentConcurrency**                                               | Observed concurrency (sampled over a window per AWS) |
| **ExecutionEnvironmentConcurrencyLimit**                                          | Ceiling per environment you configured               |
| **ExecutionEnvironmentCPUUtilization**, **ExecutionEnvironmentMemoryUtilization** | Whether that version is sized correctly              |

## Cadence and retention

Per AWS, LMI metrics are published on **5-minute** intervals and retained **15 months** — plan dashboards and alarms around that granularity.

VPC connectivity for Lambda Managed Instances
High-level connectivity patterns for LMI in your VPC — outbound access to AWS APIs, logs, and X-Ray; public subnet, VPC endpoints, and private subnet with NAT.

import { CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

Functions on LMI run in **your VPC**. You still need a deliberate path for **outbound** access: other AWS services (for example S3, DynamoDB), **CloudWatch Logs**, and **X-Ray** telemetry. Without it, those calls fail even when IAM allows them.

For step-by-step console flows and IPv4/IPv6 variants, use [Networking for Lambda Managed Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-networking.html) as the product reference.

## Connectivity pattern

| Pattern                              | Idea                                                                                                 | Typical trade-off                                                          |
| ------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Public subnet + internet gateway** | Instances reach the internet directly (IPv4 and/or IPv6; egress-only IPv6 if you want outbound-only) | Simple and low cost; mind **inbound** exposure if anything is truly public |
| **VPC endpoints**                    | Private attachment to supported AWS services; traffic stays on the AWS network                       | Strong isolation; **per-endpoint** and **per-AZ** cost; plan HA across AZs |
| **Private subnet + NAT gateway**     | Private workloads; outbound IPv4 via NAT in a public subnet                                          | Common enterprise shape; NAT **hourly and data** charges; IPv4-oriented    |

## Choosing

| If you prioritise…                            | Lean toward…                                                                                   |
| --------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Speed and cost for experiments                | Public subnet + IGW (with tight security groups)                                               |
| Production isolation from the public internet | VPC endpoints for AWS APIs you use, and/or private subnets + NAT for general egress            |
| No inbound from the internet                  | Private + NAT, or public subnet with controls; IPv6 egress-only IGW if IPv6 fits your services |

Subnet choice, route tables, security groups, and endpoint policies are part of **capacity provider** placement — see [Placement & capacity](/aws-lambda-managed-instance-walkthrough/overview/concepts/capacity-providers/).

IAM — execution role and managed-EC2 operator
Why LMI separates function execution trust from the operator trust Lambda uses to manage managed EC2 capacity in your account.

import { CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

LMI splits IAM into two distinct role domains. Keep this separation explicit so application permissions and fleet-operator permissions are reviewed independently.

## Role boundary model

| Role domain                   | Principal using it            | Scope                                                             |
| ----------------------------- | ----------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Function execution role**   | Your function runtime         | Application behavior: logs, service calls, data-plane access      |
| **Managed-EC2 operator role** | Lambda service on your behalf | Fleet operations for managed instances tied to capacity providers |

The execution role answers **what your function code can do at runtime**. The operator role answers **what the Lambda service can do in your account to create and operate capacity-provider fleet resources**.

## Trust and review checkpoints

| Checkpoint                  | What to validate                                                                                     |
| --------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Trust policy separation** | The runtime principal and Lambda operator principal are not conflated into one broad role            |
| **Least privilege**         | Execution role is limited to app/runtime needs; operator role is limited to managed-fleet operations |
| **Auditability**            | Role purpose and ownership are clear in IAM naming, tagging, and policy documentation                |
| **Operational boundary**    | Managed instances are Lambda-operated capacity, not general EC2 admin targets                        |

For policy names, service-trust details, and release-specific behavior, use [Lambda Managed Instances](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances.html) as the product reference.

High-level LMI quotas — capacity provider API rates, account resource limits, and event source mapping throughput caps versus default Lambda.

import { CardGrid, LinkCard } from "@astrojs/starlight/components";

LMI carries **separate quotas** from **default Lambda**. Some values are **fixed**; others can be **raised** through Service Quotas. Treat this page as orientation — numbers change with AWS releases.

For authoritative tables and increase procedures, use [Lambda Managed Instances quotas](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-managed-instances-quotas.html) as the product reference.

## Capacity provider API throttles

These limits apply **across all capacity-provider control-plane calls** in aggregate (create, update, describe, delete, etc.).

| Limit                       | Value (per AWS)            | Adjustable |
| --------------------------- | -------------------------- | ---------- |
| Combined **read** API rate  | **15** requests per second | No         |
| Combined **write** API rate | **1** request per second   | No         |

Plan automation (Terraform applies, CI) so you do not burst into throttling.

## Account resource quotas

| Resource                                    | Quota (per AWS) | Adjustable             |
| ------------------------------------------- | --------------- | ---------------------- |
| **Capacity providers** per account          | **1,000**       | Yes (where AWS allows) |
| **Function versions** per capacity provider | **100**         | No                     |

The per-provider version cap shapes how many published versions you can attach to a single pool of capacity.

## Event source mapping throughput

LMI supports event sources including **SQS**, **DynamoDB Streams**, **Kinesis**, **MSK**, and **self-managed Kafka**; throughput and mapping counts have **LMI-specific** caps.

| Area                              | Example quota (per AWS) | Adjustable |
| --------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ---------- |
| Standard **SQS** ESM throughput   | **5 MB/s**              | No         |
| Standard **Kafka** ESM throughput | **1 MB/s**              | No         |
| Standard **Kafka** ESM **count**  | **100** mappings        | No         |
| **Kinesis** ESM throughput        | **25 MB/s**             | Yes        |
| **DynamoDB** ESM throughput       | **10 MB/s**             | Yes        |
| **Async invoke** throughput       | **5 MB/s**              | Yes        |

Use the product page for the full matrix and any new rows.

## Requesting increases

Where AWS marks a quota **increaseable**, use the **Service Quotas** console for **AWS Lambda** and open a **quota increase** request with justification.
